synthesis:
### QE ArXiv Watch Weekly — Week ending June 29, 2026 (7 papers)

If you only lean in for one idea this week: **Bayesian experimental design is drifting from “learn the parameters fastest” toward “collect the data that makes the *next decision* best”**—and two papers make that shift feel unusually actionable. One even bakes *fairness* directly into what you choose to measure next.

#### Design is getting more “task-shaped” (and that matters in practice)

A lot of us still treat DOE like a clean, model-centric game: maximize Fisher info, minimize posterior variance, maybe approximate EIG if we’re fancy. Rossa et al. flip the framing with **ACTION-BED**: instead of optimizing “how much uncertainty we reduce,” we optimize **expected future loss** on the downstream action we actually care about.

That’s a subtle change with big implications. Imagine you’re running a reliability demonstration, troubleshooting a yield excursion, or tuning an HVAC model: you don’t win by knowing *everything*; you win by knowing the *right things* to choose the next maintenance action, setpoint policy, or mitigation.

The clever part is computational: by jointly optimizing an **action policy** and a **design policy**, they rewrite the objective to avoid the usual doubly-intractable EIG mess. When simulators are differentiable, you get pathwise gradients; otherwise, REINFORCE-style gradients still work. Benchmarks (source localization, pendulums, even masked MNIST) suggest better task loss and often less compute than strong EIG baselines. If you’ve avoided Bayesian design because it felt like “posterior estimation all the way down,” this is one of the more credible off-ramps.

Now layer on Hedman et al.’s **FairBED**, which asks a question we rarely formalize in QE: *can we design data collection to be informative for the quality/reliability target while being intentionally uninformative about a sensitive attribute?* They operationalize “leakage” as mutual information about the sensitive attribute and subtract it (weighted by β) from target EIG. In active learning settings (Census Income, Student Graduation, CelebA), the fairness–accuracy trade improves meaningfully versus standard acquisitions.

For practitioners, the takeaway isn’t “mutual information everywhere.” It’s: **fairness can be treated as a first-class constraint in sampling and measurement design**, not just a post-hoc model audit.

#### N-of-1 design: the “switching schedule” is your design variable

Guo et al. tackle a very QE-flavored setting: **one unit, lots of time, treatments that can carry over** (finite impulse response with K lags). They compare Markov “random-switch” assignments and deterministic “cycle-switch” blocks.

Two results you can actually use:

- If you care about **lag-specific effects** (which lag k mattered), **i.i.d. Bernoulli(1/2)** randomization is asymptotically optimal—and it’s also the robust/minimax choice.
- If you care about **cumulative effects** (total effect across lags), you want **longer blocks**; their block-length optimum is roughly  
  \[
  \ell^\* \approx (K-1) + \sqrt{K-1}.
  \]
This is a nice “design dial”: pick randomness for isolating dynamics; pick persistence for estimating totals.

#### Spend measurement budget where it buys variance (even if it’s “incomplete” data)

Mareis et al. offer a clean optimization story: in an IV Gaussian graphical model, you can buy **complete samples** \((X_1,X_2,X_3)\) or cheaper **partial samples** \((X_1,X_2)\). If your IV estimator is a ratio of covariances, improving the denominator with lots of cheap partials can cut variance—*but only under specific sign/strength conditions* (notably when confounding and effect signs oppose and instruments are weak). They derive the asymptotic variance when numerator/denominator come from different datasets and give closed-form allocation rules.

This feels directly transferable to: “Do we log *all* signals at high rate, or log a subset broadly and a subset deeply?”

#### Two reliability papers worth stealing ideas from

- Lucas et al. show how to estimate **failure-focused Shapley effects** when inputs are correlated and you only have a single sample of failure points. The move is to estimate conditional marginals \(f_{X_u\mid F_t}\) with **normalizing flows**, then compute target Sobol/Shapley quantities via density ratios—no extra expensive model calls. If you’re already doing conditional simulation around rare events, this is a promising sensitivity workflow upgrade.
- Failenschmid et al. make Gaussian process state-space models less of a “beautiful but fragile” tool by improving MCMC mixing (block sampling hyperparameters with dynamics) and particle filtering (auxiliary PF with better proposals), then validating with simulation-based calibration. If you use GP-SSMs for degradation or latent health states, the emphasis on calibrated inference and reliable computation is the real value.

#### The question to take into next week

As design gets more policy-based—optimizing for decisions, constraints, and even fairness—**what do we want our “design objective” to be in QE: parameter precision, action regret, cost-of-poor-quality, or something else entirely?** The tooling is catching up to whatever answer we pick.

papers.title: Experimental Design When N Equals One
Optimizing Experimental Design for Causal Effect Estimation with Partial Measurements
High-dimensional reliability-oriented Shapley effect estimation with Normalizing Flows
Active Learning for Optimal Experimental Design in Machine Learning-Based Building Energy System Identification
Learning Nonlinear Dynamics: Improving the Estimation Efficiency and Reliability of Gaussian Process State-Space Models
papers.authors: Jan I. Failenschmid|Leonie V. D. E. Vogelsmeier|Joris Mulder|Joran Jongerling

papers.title: Action-BED: Task-Driven Bayesian Experimental Design with Singly Intractable Objectives
FairBED: A Bayesian Experimental Design Approach to Gathering Fairer Data
papers.authors: Marcel Hedman|Emily Alger|Brieuc Lehmann|Chris Holmes|Tom Rainforth
